EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through during the afternoon and offshore during the early morning hours of the 17th. A few showers and thunderstorms developed during the evenings hours over inland areas of Eastern NC. One storm became severe and produced wind damage over Onslow and Duplin counties. Later in the evening, an isolated severe storm came ashore and produced wind damage over Harkers Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on powerlines at the intersection of Haw Branch Road and Highway 24. The time was estimated by radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through during the afternoon and offshore during the early morning hours of the 17th. A few showers and thunderstorms developed during the evenings hours over inland areas of Eastern NC. One storm became severe and produced wind damage over Onslow and Duplin counties. Later in the evening, an isolated severe storm came ashore and produced wind damage over Harkers Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey estimates wind speeds around 60 MPH on Harkers Island. Damage at one location consisted of a shed blown on its side, and a porch roof and steps blown about 15 to 25 feet toward the north. Some shingles were also lifted from the south side of the house towards the water. Debris was scattered across Island Road and into the parking lot of the Dollar General. This building had minor damage consisting of metal drip edging being blown upward. The debris area ended on the north side of the Dollar General building. Wind speed estimates are consistent on the lower end of the damage scale due to lack of anchoring and overall construction of the building, combined with a lack of damage outside of this specific location.

A strong cold front crossed the Appalachian Mountains on March 5. Strong winds aloft were mixed down to the surface from showers and thunderstorms, which caused notable reports of damaging winds that blew down numerous trees and power lines. The most notable damage reported was a roof and a metal carport blown down by the thunderstorm winds in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down one tree near the intersection of U.S. Highway 158 and Witty Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region during the afternoon and evening. Ahead of it, gusty south winds were observed across Eastern NC. A band of showers and thunderstorms produced enhanced wind gusts to severe levels during the afternoon and early evening hours, with widespread gusts between 55 and 65 mph, with sporadic higher gusts. Some isolated small hail was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 center reported the entire island of Ocracoke was without power. The time was estimated based on the peak wind gust at Ocracoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong mid to upper level low pressure system moved from the southern Plains northeast through the Great Lakes region March 4-5. Ahead of a trailing cold front, a long and nearly continuous line of showers and storms which had held together crossing the Mid South region, pushed into the North Carolina Foothills and western Piedmont in the morning of the 5th, crossing central North Carolina through mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across the city of Winston-Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep mid-level trough and associated cold front approached from the west on the 16th. Ahead of the trough/front, surface dewpoints in the 60s spread into central North Carolina, with strong low-level and deep-layer shear. Two rounds of severe weather occurred from mid morning through the evening hours. Several reports of damaging winds and large hail were reported, along with isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported, with a size of 1.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavy, broken line of prefrontal storms draped across the lower Tennessee Valley and Southeast shifted into and through the Carolinas during the afternoon into the evening. The lead band of storms weakened significantly as it shifted into the southern Piedmont, but discrete thunderstorms ahead of the line moved into the Triangle, initially producing narrow swaths of sub-severe hail and damaging winds, before congealing into a singular bowing segment as it moved into the northern and central Coastal Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down along the roadway of Lower River Road and Ragan Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong S/SE winds developed across western North Carolina mountains during the late evening of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th in advance of a strong frontal system. Brief periods of damaging wind gusts were reported across portions of Macon and Graham Counties, resulting in numerous downed trees and power lines, scattered power outages, and minor structural damage. Winds remained strong as they shifted to the WNW in the wake of the front during the daylight hours. This resulted in some additional tree damage, with one tree falling on a recreational vehicle and seriously injuring an occupant in western McDowell County.

A cold front approached the Appalachian Mountains on March 16. An isolated thunderstorm developed in Rockingham County and became severe. The storm produced hail up to the size of quarters at Reidsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell at Reidsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong S/SE winds developed across western North Carolina mountains during the late evening of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th in advance of a strong frontal system. Brief periods of damaging wind gusts were reported across portions of Macon and Graham Counties, resulting in numerous downed trees and power lines, scattered power outages, and minor structural damage. An area of showers with isolated embedded thunderstorms moved across the North Carolina Piedmont during the morning in advance of a cold front. A brief EF1 tornado developed in association with an embedded line segment in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found the path of an EF1 tornado that began in the vicinity of Porter Ridge High School. Tree branches were broken and a free-standing light pole was  broken off at the base. The fence around the schools tennis court was also blown over and two storage buildings collapsed nearby. The tornado moved northeast   across Ridge Road, where a house under construction was heavily damaged, presumably due to air rushing in via window and garage door openings, which lifted off part of the roof. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped along Friendship Baptist Church Rd. Minor structural damage occurred to a house in the Benton Acres neighborhood. The tornado continued northeast to Lawyers Rd just west of US 601, snapping several hundred yards of wooden power poles, while causing little damage to adjacent homes. The tornado apparently dissipated at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a strong cold front exited the Mid Atlantic on March 6, northwest winds increased and became gusty. These high winds blew down numerous trees that knocked down power lines. The highest gust recorded was 60 mph in Watauga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station one mile west of Aho measured a gust of 60 mph. In addition, another weather station one mile southeast of Boone measured a gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a strong cold front exited the Mid Atlantic on March 6, northwest winds increased and became gusty. These high winds blew down numerous trees that knocked down power lines. The highest gust recorded was 60 mph in Watauga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down numerous trees that fell across roads and also knocked down power lines across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the Appalachian Mountains on the morning of March 16 due to a deepening area of low pressure across the Great Lakes. The intense pressure gradient along the approaching frontal boundary triggered strong south winds that blew down numerous trees across Alleghany, Surry, and Watauga counties. A roof was also damaged by the winds in Boone. The highest wind gust reported was 75 mph from a mesonet station at Banner Elk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down several trees across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavy, broken line of prefrontal storms draped across the lower Tennessee Valley and Southeast shifted into and through the Carolinas during the afternoon into the evening. The lead band of storms weakened significantly as it shifted into the southern Piedmont, but discrete thunderstorms ahead of the line moved into the Triangle, initially producing narrow swaths of sub-severe hail and damaging winds, before congealing into a singular bowing segment as it moved into the northern and central Coastal Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Asbury Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm system that produce a deadly tornado outbreak over the Southeast U.S. and Mid-Mississippi Valley moved across the Carolinas causing some severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per media, Wilmington FD responded to a lightning related fire on Shoal Bay Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Midwest and surface low near the Great Lakes brought a robust cold front into the Mid Atlantic region. A strongly-forced area of linear convection moved from west to east across portions of northeast North Carolina. These storms mostly produced strong to severe straight line wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down across Gates county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Midwest and surface low near the Great Lakes brought a robust cold front into the Mid Atlantic region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region, with a tight pressure gradient existing between it and a surface high departing off the eastern United States coast. This pressure gradient led to strong, gusty winds over the area, especially across the higher elevations. During the day of January 29th, a west-northwesterly low level jet also moved eastward into the Piedmont, and contributed to more strengthening of the winds. Wind gusts of 60 mph and greater resulted in a few hundred power outages in the northwest North Carolina mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 71 mph gust was measured at the Ashe County Airport, and a 69 mph gust was measured near Baldwin. Over 720 customers in Ashe County had lost power by the morning of January 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system tracked across the eastern United States through the day of January 11 and into the overnight, while the resultant surface low moved from the Gulf Coast into the Carolina coast. Some initial precipitation spread over North Carolina from the surface low, but the heavier precipitation occurred with the passage of the upper level storm system. Strong lift resulted in some banding of heavy snow with localized areas of higher accumulations of northwest North Carolina. The accumulating snow led to hazardous road conditions and some vehicle accidents, a few of which resulted in road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3.5 and 7 inches fell across Ashe County from January 10 through January 11. The highest amount of seven inches was reported in West Jefferson. Key Impacts: power outages, vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high shear/low instability line of thunderstorms ahead of a cold front caused isolated wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the northeast Gulf and off the southeast coast of the United States tracked off the coast of the Carolinas. This system combined with Arctic air brought snowfall across the Piedmont and northern Coastal Plain and freezing rain over the Sandhills to southern Coastal Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county received about a tenth of an inch of freezing rain, as well as a half inch or less of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds at the surface and just aloft spread into the area in conjunction with a coastal warm front lifting northwest through the area, ahead of a cold front and upper level trough approaching from the west. Background strong and gusty winds from the south and southwest prompted a high wind warning (northeast zones) and wind advisory (elsewhere), but the strongest winds were associated with a prefrontal line of storms that crossed the area during the morning into very early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down at the intersection of Old Highway 29 and Turner Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of several weather features resulted in moderate snowfall accumulations across the mountains, especially over Watauga County in northwest North Carolina. A strong upper level storm system rotated through a broader upper level trough that was tracking east through the central US, while a second upper level storm system moved into the Ohio Valley, which spawned a surface low pressure system that deepened as it moved off the Georgia Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between four to six inches of snow fell across Watauga County, though some higher accumulations were reported in higher elevation locations, such as in Aho, where eight inches of snow was reported, and in Sugar Grove, where 6.5 inches were reported. Key Impacts: power outages, slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure cyclone passing to the south with upper level forcing induced a broad and significant snowstorm across the region, including southwest North Carolina, which experienced heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow followed by light sleet and wintry mix yielded 3-4 of snow across Cherokee County, per reports from both a trained spotter and several public reports in Hot House, Murphy, Marble, and Culberson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure cyclone passing to the south with upper level forcing induced a broad and significant snowstorm across the region, including southwest North Carolina, which experienced heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow followed by several hours of sleet and freezing rain led to a compaction of the snowpack. Observer took several samples before averaging and yielded 3.4 inches of snow and ice pack outside of Hayesville, NC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe convective showers moved through Eastern NC during the mid to late morning, exiting the Outer Banks early in the afternoon. Though no lightning occurred, severe convective winds gusted to 60 mph or greater, producing sporadic to at times widespread tree damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tri-County Electric COOP reports power outages due to trees and powerlines blown down near LaGrange along and near Highway 70.

Stronger winds and temperatures at or below 0 caused wind chills of -20 or colder above 5000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred over the Mid-Atlantic February 19-20 2025. Arctic 1050mb high pressure built into the area early on the 19th. Two mid level disturbances began to phase over eastern North Carolina with periods of heavy snowfall featuring 1 to 2 inch per hour snowfall rates. Very cold temperatures resulted in mostly snow across the region, though some sleet did mix in near the Albemarle Sound. Snow totals ranged from around 5 inches for areas west of Interstate 95 to 14 inches in Currituck County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports across the county were sparse but generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches. A CoCoRaHS report of 5 inches of snow was received from 1 WSW of Windsor, NC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and lift associated with low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast overspread the Southeast and combined with cold surface high pressure to result in development of snow across the southern North Carolina foothills during the late morning. Snow steadily accumulated, with amounts of around an inch reported by mid-afternoon. Precipitation then began to transition to sleet and freezing rain as temperatures warmed aloft. By early evening, sleet and ice accumulation of 1/2 inch to near an inch was reported across much of the area.

An upper level storm system moved southeast through the Rockies, then east across the Southern U.S. on January 21-22. This storm system overlapped with an usually cold airmass already in place across much of the U.S., leading to a rare deep south winter storm that blanketed Eastern North Carolina with at least several inches of snow. Snow began during the evening hours of the 21st as a surface trough moved into the Coastal Plain with snowfall then spreading east as the evening progressed and a stationary front set up offshore. Snow became heavy at times especially across portions of the Outer Banks where the highest snowfall amounts fell. Snow then quickly ended across the Coastal Plain on the morning of the 22nd while snow stuck around the OBX and eastern Carteret County until the afternoon on the 22nd. The winter storm was responsible for producing a swath of 2-6 of snow for much of ENC, with several locations across the Outer Banks reporting amounts as high as 6-9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread public reports of six to nine inches of snowfall. Snow fell between the evening of the 21st and the morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough and offshore low pressure combined with cold air already in place due to Arctic high pressure to bring two to six inches of snow and sleet to southeast NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow and sleet mix fell across Bladen county, with a maximum of 6.5 measured in Kelly, NC. County schools and offices were closed for two to three days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough and offshore low pressure combined with cold air already in place due to Arctic high pressure to bring two to six inches of snow and sleet to southeast NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around four inches of snow and sleet mix fell. Traffic accident involving multiple vehicles occurred in Hampstead, NC. Multiple traffic accidents reported and a few vehicles needed assistance due to sliding off the road. County offices were closed for two days and schools were closed on the 22nd.

Moisture and lift associated with low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast overspread the Southeast and combined with cold surface high pressure to result in development of snow and sleet across the southern North Carolina Piedmont during early afternoon. The precipitation gradually accumulated, with half inch to an inch of sleet and snow reported across the area by early evening. At that point, warming temperatures aloft resulted in a transition to freezing rain, which persisted through the evening before tapering off during the early morning. Total ice accretion of around 1/4 inch was reported on top of the sleet and snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough over much of the country brought cold air to our region, which when coupled with a weak surface low passing by to our south, resulted in accumulating snow and ice for the area.  Snowfall totals ranged from less than an inch across the western Piedmont and Triad to as much as 2 to 4 inches across the Coastal Plain and I-95 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 3 inches county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front brought strong northwest winds and reinforced very cold air over the North Carolina mountains during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Temperatures in the single digits and teens and winds gusting from 15 to 25 mph resulted in minimum wind chill values of 0 to -10 during this time. Stronger winds and temperatures at or below 0 caused wind chills of -15 or colder above 5000 feet.

Snow showers developed on the western slopes of the southern Appalachians during later morning in response to strong northwest upslope flow in the wake of a cold front. The snow showers continued off and on through the daylight hours of the 3rd before tapering off during the late afternoon and evening. Notable accumulations were primarily confined to the higher elevations along the North Carolina/Tennessee border, where 3 to 5 inches were reported. However, even some valley locations received as much as two inches.

A cold northwest upslope flow developing in the wake of an arctic cold front resulted in development of snow showers across the North Carolina mountains by late morning of the 19th. Snow showers continued off and on through the day before tapering off the flurries during the early morning of the 20th. Total accumulations were generally 3-6 inches across the high elevations along the Tennessee border. Lower elevations northwest of a line from the Pigeon River drainage through far northern Buncombe County through the northern mountains received anywhere from 0.5 inch up to 2 inches with locally higher amounts.

Precipitation developed across western North Carolina during the early morning hours as moisture and lift associated with an area of low pressure and associated frontal system over the Tennessee Valley overspread the region. An area of freezing temperatures near the Blue Ridge escarpment across the mountains...and along and north of I-40 across the foothills and Piedmont resulted in wintry weather falling across those locations. The primary precipitation type was freezing rain, with anywhere from trace amounts to up to a tenth of an inch reported by late morning, mainly on elevated surfaces. However, some snow fell across the higher mountains, with around an inch reported in a few locations.

At the surface, the center of a strong arctic high (1040mb) extending from the central CONUS to the East Coast gradually moved from the Mississippi Valley on January 21 to the East Coast on January 22. Meanwhile, an area of low pressure formed in central Gulf along a stationary front extending from the central Gulf to offshore of the East Coast. Aloft, a sharpening but positively tiled shortwave trough crossed the area during the evening of the January 21 through the early morning of January 22. A very cold and dry low-level Arctic airmass was in place over the entire area with temperatures in the lower-mid 20s (with dew points around 0F) during the afternoon on January 22 before any snow had started to fall. Even though the surface low tracked well to the southeast of the area, mid to upper level forcing for ascent was strong given decent warm air advection aloft. The sloping mid to upper level frontogenesis zone was over the area, with the strongest frontogenesis in the dendritic growth zone (700-550mb) residing over southeast Virginia during the evening into early overnight hours on January 21. Despite the very cold and dry antecedent airmass, light snow began falling as far west as the Richmond Metro by 7 PM. The low-levels then quickly saturated area-wide by 8-9 PM. Most of the snow was light in intensity, but a band of moderate to heavy snow (likely with 1-2���/hour rates) set up across extreme southeast Virginia and northeast North Carolina on the warm side of the mid-level frontogenesis zone. In addition, given the very cold temperatures (and forcing for ascent concentrated in the dendritic growth zone), snow-to-liquid ratios were 15-20:1 even across northeast North Carolina. Also, given the cold low-level temperatures (925mb temperatures of -13 to -16C) and the northerly flow, some additional enhancement of the snow was observed across Norfolk into portions of interior northeast North Carolina due to moisture flow off the bay. The steady snow ended between midnight and 4 AM on January 22 as the shortwave quickly pushed offshore, but totals across northeast North Carolina ranged from 3-7 inches (locally 8 inches). Southern portions of Hampton Roads saw around 3 inches, with localized totals around 4 inches in that area of Norfolk that saw bay-enhanced snow. After the steady synoptic snow ended, a bay-effect snow band developed given minimal shear from the surface to 900mb (with the top of that layer in the dendritic growth zone). In addition, the difference between the water temperature (38F/3C) and the 900mb temperature (around -15C) was more than enough for a few hundred J/kg of bay-induced instability. This combination of factors set the stage for a true bay effect snow event that likely produced localized snow totals of 0.5 inches on top of what had already fallen. The bay effect band drifted west and weakened by 5-6 AM as the fetch over the bay was disrupted with the low-level flow shifting from due north to north-northeast. However, another band of bay-effect snow showers developed from late morning into the afternoon on January 22 near the Norfolk area before tapering off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches across Gates County. A snowfall total of 5 inches was reported 2 miles (SW) from Easons Crossroads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air associated with arctic high pressure settled over the North Carolina mountains during the early morning hours of the 9th. Temperatures in the single digits and teens and winds gusting from 10 to 20 mph resulted in minimum wind chill values of 0 to -10 during this time. Stronger winds and temperatures at or below 0 caused wind chills of -15 or colder above 5000 feet.

Meanwhile, an area of low pressure formed in central Gulf along a stationary front extending from the central Gulf to offshore of the East Coast. Aloft, a sharpening but positively tiled shortwave trough crossed the area during the evening of the January 21 through the early morning of January 22. A very cold and dry low-level Arctic airmass was in place over the entire area with temperatures in the lower-mid 20s (with dew points around 0F) during the afternoon on January 22 before any snow had started to fall. Even though the surface low tracked well to the southeast of the area, mid to upper level forcing for ascent was strong given decent warm air advection aloft. The sloping mid to upper level frontogenesis zone was over the area, with the strongest frontogenesis in the dendritic growth zone (700-550mb) residing over southeast Virginia during the evening into early overnight hours on January 21. Despite the very cold and dry antecedent airmass, light snow began falling as far west as the Richmond Metro by 7 PM. The low-levels then quickly saturated area-wide by 8-9 PM. Most of the snow was light in intensity, but a band of moderate to heavy snow (likely with 1-2���/hour rates) set up across extreme southeast Virginia and northeast North Carolina on the warm side of the mid-level frontogenesis zone. In addition, given the very cold temperatures (and forcing for ascent concentrated in the dendritic growth zone), snow-to-liquid ratios were 15-20:1 even across northeast North Carolina. Also, given the cold low-level temperatures (925mb temperatures of -13 to -16C) and the northerly flow, some additional enhancement of the snow was observed across Norfolk into portions of interior northeast North Carolina due to moisture flow off the bay. The steady snow ended between midnight and 4 AM on January 22 as the shortwave quickly pushed offshore, but totals across northeast North Carolina ranged from 3-7 inches (locally 8 inches). Southern portions of Hampton Roads saw around 3 inches, with localized totals around 4 inches in that area of Norfolk that saw bay-enhanced snow. After the steady synoptic snow ended, a bay-effect snow band developed given minimal shear from the surface to 900mb (with the top of that layer in the dendritic growth zone). In addition, the difference between the water temperature (38F/3C) and the 900mb temperature (around -15C) was more than enough for a few hundred J/kg of bay-induced instability. This combination of factors set the stage for a true bay effect snow event that likely produced localized snow totals of 0.5 inches on top of what had already fallen. The bay effect band drifted west and weakened by 5-6 AM as the fetch over the bay was disrupted with the low-level flow shifting from due north to north-northeast. However, another band of bay-effect snow showers developed from late morning into the afternoon on January 22 near the Norfolk area before tapering off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 6 inches across Perquimans. A snowfall total of 6.2 inches was reported 1 mile (NNW) from Chapanoke.

Stronger winds and temperatures at or below 0 caused wind chills of -15 or colder above 5000 feet.

Precipitation developed across western North Carolina during the early morning hours as moisture and lift associated with an area of low pressure and associated frontal system over the Tennessee Valley overspread the region. An area of freezing temperatures near the Blue Ridge escarpment across the mountains...and along and north of I-40 across the foothills and Piedmont resulted in wintry weather falling across those locations. The primary precipitation type was freezing rain, with anywhere from trace amounts to up to a tenth of an inch reported by late morning, mainly on elevated surfaces. However, some snow fell across the higher mountains, with around an inch reported in a few locations.

An upper level storm system moved southeast through the Rockies, then east across the Southern U.S. on January 21-22. This storm system overlapped with an usually cold airmass already in place across much of the U.S., leading to a rare deep south winter storm that blanketed Eastern North Carolina with at least several inches of snow. Snow began during the evening hours of the 21st as a surface trough moved into the Coastal Plain with snowfall then spreading east as the evening progressed and a stationary front set up offshore. Snow became heavy at times especially across portions of the Outer Banks where the highest snowfall amounts fell. Snow then quickly ended across the Coastal Plain on the morning of the 22nd while snow stuck around the OBX and eastern Carteret County until the afternoon on the 22nd. The winter storm was responsible for producing a swath of 2-6 of snow for much of ENC, with several locations across the Outer Banks reporting amounts as high as 6-9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread reports of three to five inches of snowfall fell across the county between the evening of the 21st and the morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the high wind event from 12/31/2024 of strong winds behind a departing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured at the Ashe County Airport, and is one report from a high wind event that began on December 31st, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system tracked from the Ohio Valley into the Central Appalachians Thursday into Friday. The associated upper  level disturbance enhanced lift across the area, resulting in snow beginning to fall on the morning of January 3rd and continuing through the day.|Bands of brief heavy snow brought localized higher amounts of snow, and northwest winds behind the passage of the clipper system resulted in upslope flow and additional snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between two and three inches of snow fell across Alleghany County between 3 PM and 11 PM EST. There were at least 20 different vehicle incidents, including vehicles sliding off into ditches or into guardrails, due to slick roads across the county. Two vehicles were overturned on North Carolina State Highway 18. Key Impacts: traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought numerous impacts to North Carolina Sunday January 5th and into Monday January 6th. A strong surface low pressure system moved northeastward from the Central Plains and into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, with the associated warm front lifting northward from the southeast into the lower Mid Atlantic. Precipitation began as snow, but strong warm air advection ahead of the low pressure center, and behind the warm front, led to a transition to all liquid precipitation. Surface temperatures remained below freezing, and so a prolonged period of freezing rain led to ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch overnight and during the Monday morning commute. Hundreds had lost power by the morning of January 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to an inch of snow fell across the county from January 5th through January 6th. Between 0.10 and 0.15 inches of ice accumulated as well. Key Impacts: traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked from eastern Ohio across Pennsylvania and into New England on February 16, pushing a strong cold front across the region. A warm front lifted north across the region ahead of the cold front with a strong low level jet allowing for strong southwest winds to develop across portions of northeast North Carolina on February 16. A west wind developed in the afternoon behind the cold front and remained gusty, however, winds were generally 35 to 40 mph in the afternoon as opposed to 50 to 60 mph ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds developed behind a warm front on February 16. The wind gusts generally remained between 35 and 40 miles per hour throughout much of the day. However, a CWOP mesonet in Chapanoke measured a peak wind gust of 58 miles per hour (50 knots) late in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and isolated thunderstorms moved into the North Carolina mountains during the evening ahead of a cold front. One of the storms produced penny to ping pong ball size hail along the Madison/Buncombe County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public measured ping-pong ball sized hail near the intersection of Meadows Town Rd and Sandy Mush Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation overspread the Carolina mountains and North Carolina foothills during the evening of the 11th in association with an approaching frontal system. While temperatures were above freezing across much of the area, a pocket of cold air developed along the Blue Ridge escarpment, resulting in precipitation falling as freezing rain there for a prolonged period. Total ice accretion was mainly in the 0.10-0.25 range across this area, although some pockets along the continental divide saw more than 0.25 inch, with isolated to scattered power outages reported. Meanwhile, areas across the western halves of Buncombe, Henderson, Yancey, and  Mitchell Counties, and much of the southern half of Caldwell and Alexander Counties only received rain.

A line of severe convective showers moved through Eastern NC during the mid to late morning, exiting the Outer Banks early in the afternoon. Though no lightning occurred, severe convective winds gusted to 60 mph or greater, producing sporadic to at times widespread tree damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted or snapped. The report was from mPING.

An area of low pressure over the northeast Gulf and off the southeast coast of the United States tracked off the coast of the Carolinas. This system combined with Arctic air brought snowfall across the Piedmont and northern Coastal Plain and freezing rain over the Sandhills to southern Coastal Plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure wedge was situated over the region as a surface low pressure system developed over the southern Plains, with an expansive area of precipitation that shifted eastward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of February 11. Precipitation began as snow, but with the high pressure wedge in place and warm air increasing aloft, a transition from snow to sleet and freezing rain occurred along and south of the North Carolina and Virginia border. Ice accumulations on trees and power lines resulted in thousands of power outages and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 0.15 and 0.75 inches of ice accumulated across Watauga County from February 11 through February 12. The highest amount of 0.75 inches was reported just southeast of Deep Gap. Over 5600 customers were reported without power by the mid afternoon of February 12. Key Impacts: power outages, downed trees, road closures.

An area of low pressure developed along the U.S. Gulf Coast, traveled northeast up along the Southeast U.S. coastline, then off the coast of the Carolinas during the time period of February 19th to the early morning hours of February 20th. As this occurred, an area of widespread precipitation developed along the track of the low. Where temperatures were cold enough, there was a mix of sleet, freezing rain, and snow. For areas from the Crystal Coast through the southern Outer Banks, the precipitation type was mostly rain. This event produced a swath of accumulating sleet and snow from central North Carolina through southeast Virginia. South of the snow and sleet, there was a swath of accumulating ice, with a zone of 0.10 - 0.30 of ice accretion. The combination of ice and wind led to tree and power line damage across parts of ENC, with numerous power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of public, trained spotter, and emergency management reported a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow impacting the county during the day and into the overnight hours. Widespread snow and sleet reports of two to five inches occurred with freezing rain accumulations of 0.10-0.25 inches noted as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 0.25 and 0.50 inches of ice accumulated across Alleghany County February 11 through February 12. An emergency responder was injured while clearing trees during the storm. Key Impacts: power outages, downed trees, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air and gusty winds from the northwest overspread the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 20th in the wake of an arctic cold front. Air temperatures in the single digits and teens combined with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph to produce wind chills of 0 to -10 in the valleys, and -10 to -20 above 3500 feet. Even colder temperatures and stronger winds produced wind chill of -20 and colder above 5000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air and gusty winds from the northwest overspread the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 20th in the wake of an arctic cold front. Air temperatures in the single digits and teens combined with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph to produce wind chills of 0 to -10 in the valleys, and -10 to -20 above 3500 feet. Even colder temperatures and stronger winds produced wind chill of -20 and colder above 5000 feet.

An area of low pressure developed along the U.S. Gulf Coast, traveled northeast up along the Southeast U.S. coastline, then off the coast of the Carolinas during the time period of February 19th to the early morning hours of February 20th. As this occurred, an area of widespread precipitation developed along the track of the low. Where temperatures were cold enough, there was a mix of sleet, freezing rain, and snow. For areas from the Crystal Coast through the southern Outer Banks, the precipitation type was mostly rain. This event produced a swath of accumulating sleet and snow from central North Carolina through southeast Virginia. South of the snow and sleet, there was a swath of accumulating ice, with a zone of 0.10 - 0.30 of ice accretion. The combination of ice and wind led to tree and power line damage across parts of ENC, with numerous power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread ice accretions of 0.15 to 0.25 inches were reported. The reports came from Half Moon, Richlands, Jacksonville, Swansboro, and Hubert. Jones/Onslow Electric COOP reported multiple trees that were downed due to the freezing rain accretion resulting in over 2500 power outages across the county. In addition to this, up to 0.1 inches of sleet fell across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado developed just inside Edgecombe County north of the town of Fountain NC. The tornado traveled north, crossing route 258 and causing structural damage to a mobile home along with several snapped trees. It then entered a densely wooded, inaccessible area and it is unclear if the tornado remained on the ground continuously, but additional tree damage was found near the intersection of Otter Creek Church Rd and Wooten Rd. No additional damage was noted downwind of that intersection.

The tornado continued northward across a field, resulting in additional structural and tree damage. The tornado skipped erratically to the northwest, eventually causing damage to trees and a house along Faith Baptist Church Rd, before dissipating in a field nearby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm moved into southeast Martin County before moving northeastward through Martin and far western Washington Counties. As the thunderstorm moved through Martin County, a tornado touched down in an open field southwest of Jamesville. In this area, a trained storm spotter captured the touchdown with a drone. The drone footage shows a well-defined low-level mesocyclone and a loosely-organized condensation funnel that touched the ground. The supercell thunderstorm then moved northeast through Jamesville and into far western Washington County. Here there were numerous reports of funnel clouds relayed to the National Weather Service via the county 911 center. It is unknown whether the tornado |touched down at any additional point after the initial touchdown report. Radar data showed strengthening low-level rotation after the initial report, suggesting a high likelihood of additional brief touchdowns. However, there isn't conclusive evidence of |additional touchdowns, and no damage has been reported at any point along the supercell's path.||On rare occasions, it is impossible to rate the strength of a confirmed tornado because this is little to no damage evidence. As of this afternoon(Tuesday May 6th, 2025), this tornado has not produced any confirmed damage therefore per Directive: 10-1605, |this tornado has a rating of EF-U. The start coordinates were estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado touched down Thursday evening just south of|Wanchese on Roanoke Island. Video of the tornado was sent to the|office of a brief touchdown over the baseball fields of Pigum|Walker Park. The path consisted mainly of a mix of medium and|large limbs across the field. A bleacher was tossed about 10 to 15|feet into a concessions building where it then lifted toward the|parking lot. The concessions building lost shingles on both the|south and north side of the building, a strong indication of |rotation in this area, as confirmed by the video submitted to the |office. Peak wind speeds here were estimated at around 80 MPH.||A narrow and sporadic damage path continued to the southeast|across Brinkly Road, and near Old Schoolhouse Road. Damage here|again consisted of mainly medium sized limbs, but a few larger |limbs were noted. One tree was snapped halfway up the base but|were likely weaker due to disease in the middle, with winds |estimated around 75 MPH.||The path continued toward the southeast on Holly Lane, where a |medium, healthy tree was uprooted. Numerous medium to large |branches were found all along the path. While the tree was |uprooted to the south, numerous tree branches were bent back |toward the right of the path (or toward the east), confirming |rotation continued in this area. The damage path finally ended |near E R Daniels Road, where a shed was blown over, a few more |medium limbs were broken, and some horse training equipment was |blown over. Winds here likely weakened to 70 MPH.

Some of these developed into supercell thunderstorms that resulted in numerous reports of large hail along with isolated damaging wind gusts, especially across the foothills. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms over South Carolina in association with a mid-level system tracked into central North Carolina in the afternoon and evening hours. The storms initially produced a wind damage and isolated hail threat. Thereafter, the event evolved into a flash flood threat, with training and back building storms resulting in 3 or more inches per hour rainfall rates from Albemarle to Greensboro to Burlington to Henderson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nicholas Street at Epsom Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred over the Mid-Atlantic February 19-20 2025. Arctic 1050mb high pressure built into the area early on the 19th. Two mid level disturbances began to phase over eastern North Carolina with periods of heavy snowfall featuring 1 to 2 inch per hour snowfall rates. Very cold temperatures resulted in mostly snow across the region, though some sleet did mix in near the Albemarle Sound. Snow totals ranged from around 5 inches for areas west of Interstate 95 to 14 inches in Currituck County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county. The emergency manager measured 8 inches of snow in Harrellsville, NC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong mid to upper level low pressure system moved from the southern Plains northeast through the Great Lakes region March 4-5. Ahead of a trailing cold front, a long and nearly continuous line of showers and storms which had held together crossing the Mid South region, pushed into the North Carolina Foothills and western Piedmont in the morning of the 5th, crossing central North Carolina through mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported multiple trees down in Chatham County including one down in Siler City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on the night of March 16 into the early morning hours of March 17 across northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team determined that a tornado touched down just southwest of Longview Estates near Woodville Road in Woodville, North Carolina around 302 AM EDT. The tornado quickly moved northeast, rolling and destroying 3 mobile homes in Longview Estates, and damaging several others. Eight injuries occurred in Longview Estates, and the strongest wind speeds of up to 110 mph were in this area. After exiting Longview Estates, the tornado then moved across the Little River and continued into Pasquotank County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong S/SE winds developed across the North Carolina mountains early in the morning in advance of a dynamic frontal system. The strongest winds occurred between 3 AM and 5 AM, possibly assisted by a band of rain with embedded heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms that mixed higher winds from aloft to the valley floors. Some minor structural damage was reported to houses across the region, while numerous trees were blown down, some onto structures, vehicles, and power lines. One tree fell on a trailer in the Conestee community of Transylvania County, killing a 13-year-old male and an 11-year-old male who were sleeping inside. Multiple outbuildings were damaged or destroyed in Haywood County. Power outages numbered in the tens of thousands at the peak of the event. The highest reliable measured gust across the area was at the Asheville Regional Airport, where official NWS obs measured a gust of 59 mph at 422 AM. However, damage reports suggest gusts likely reached as high as 70 mph in some locations. The band of showers and storms strengthened as it moved into the Piedmont later in the morning, with a small area of damaging wind gusts developing in northeast Union County in association with a short line of storms.

Strong S/SE winds developed across western North Carolina mountains during the late evening of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th in advance of a strong frontal system. Brief periods of damaging wind gusts were reported across portions of Macon and Graham Counties, resulting in numerous downed trees and power lines, scattered power outages, and minor structural damage. Winds remained strong as they shifted to the WNW in the wake of the front during the daylight hours. This resulted in some additional tree damage, with one tree falling on a recreational vehicle and seriously injuring an occupant in western McDowell County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team determined that a tornado touched down just southwest of Longview Estates near Woodville Road in Perquimans County around 302 AM EDT. The tornado quickly moved northeast, crossing the Little River and continuing in Pasquotank County. The tornado crossed Halls Creek Road near Glade Road. The tornado tracked near Glade Road while downing numerous trees and damaging some sheds along the path. The tornado lifted around 306 AM EDT near the intersection of Glade Road and Simpson Ditch Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported multiple trees blown down on power lines and blocking roads in the Spruce Pine area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved into western North Carolina from Tennessee during the early morning hours. Multiple reports of damaging wind gusts were received, along with isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ham radio operator reported large tree limbs blown down in the Fletcher area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow ahead of a cold front across the Ohio Valley increased moisture across the Mid Atlantic, and some clearing allowed CAPE to increase to the 500 to 1000 J/kg range in the Piedmont. However, more cloud cover throughout the morning hindered destabilization somewhat, which limited coverage of storms. Enough shear in the atmosphere did allow some storms to grow stronger, and resulted in some scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees near the intersection of Highway 65 and Easley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level disturbance tracked east across the central and southern Appalachians, coinciding with the peak heating and instability during the afternoon hours. Some storms first developed along the Blue Ridge prior to the convection associated with the upper trough, while storms associated with the trough tracked into the area from the west during the mid to late afternoon. Moderate instability with MUCAPE in the 1000-1500 J/kg, and 0-6km shear was between 35-40 knots, with lapse rate increasing to 7 C/km by the afternoon, so all parameters for severe weather were favorable, and both wind damage and hail were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of 0.75 inches in diameter was observed for a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level disturbance tracked east across the central and southern Appalachians, coinciding with the peak heating and instability during the afternoon hours. Moderate instability with MUCAPE in the 1000-1500 J/kg, and 0-6km shear was between 35-40 knots, with lapse rate increasing to 7 C/km by the afternoon, so all parameters for severe weather were favorable, and both wind damage and hail were reported.

Numerous thunderstorms developed across the southern Appalachians throughout the afternoon and early evening. Some of these developed into supercell thunderstorms that resulted in numerous reports of large hail along with isolated damaging wind gusts, especially across the foothills. Storms had a tendency to redevelop over the mountains and move over roughly the same areas, especially in Rutherford County, which experienced multiple severe storms throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Asheville Regional Airport measured a wind gust of 58 mph (50 kts).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward through the day, coinciding with the passage of an upper jet streak, with the right entrance region moving over the area. This provided enough lift to trigger scattered convection during the afternoon, as daytime heating increased instability, MUCAPE reaching about 500 J/kg. Bulk shear was sufficient for some storm organization, around 30 knots, but some more cloud cover limited storm coverage, and only a few developed in the North Carolina foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple branches and a few large trees on Spainhour Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eastward moving cold front led to a line of showers and embedded storms tracking east in the early morning hours of the 4th of May. An isolated storm became tornadic in Johnston County. Additional isolated storms developed along the stalled front during the evening hours, producing some quarter inch hail in Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief but strong EF-0 tornado occurred during the early morning hours of Sunday May 4, 2025 as a line of storms moved through central North Carolina. A car port was picked up and displaced approximately 100 yards downstream, while a portion of a roof was torn off an outbuilding and displaced 100 yards to the northwest. Several weakened trees on the property were snapped and one was uprooted. The tornado was on the ground for less than a minute before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low northwest of the area resulted in strong southwesterly flow aloft over the Mid-Atlantic. Scattered to numerous strong storms formed during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines fell down due to strong winds from storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the lower Mississippi valley region brought waves of showers and embedded storms across central North Carolina. One storm cell became tornadic over Edgecombe County in the late afternoon hours, producing EF1 tornado damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down in the Southern Terrace area of Princeville, NC, during the afternoon hours on Monday May 12, 2025, and traveled north into a farm field in Tarboro. The tornado damaged a farm outbuilding along with several irrigation pivots, snapping several large trees along the way. The tornado then continued north along Wooten Street before dissipating near Elm Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level low continued moving into the central Appalachians, though weakening as it moved eastward. Although SBCAPE increased to the 1000-2000 J/kg by the afternoon, bulk shear was only around 20 knots, so coverage of strong to severe storms was limited to the Piedmont. Some of the storms became strong enough to produce some wind damage in Caswell County, North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree on Quick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms over South Carolina in association with a mid-level system tracked into central North Carolina in the afternoon and evening hours. Thereafter, the event evolved into a flash flood threat, with training and back building storms resulting in 3 or more inches per hour rainfall rates from Albemarle to Greensboro to Burlington to Henderson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported on Bunny Road near David Robinson Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure across Missouri brought deep and copious moisture across the southeastern United States and the Carolinas. Scattered to numerous showers and storms developed in the early afternoon and peaked in the early evening hours before waning by the late evening. Storms merged with an inland penetrating sea-breeze, leading to several flash flooding reports in Moore and Stanly Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morganton Road at Beulah Hill Road South was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee trough across western North Carolina and a cold front digging south from the Ohio Valley led to the development of a line of scattered showers and storms along the North Carolina and Virginia border and across the western Piedmont of North Carolina. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance out of South Carolina brought a batch of showers and storms into the Sandhills and Coastal Plain of central North Carolina during the afternoon. This led to a few storms producing wind damage in Cumberland county. Additionally, a stalled front over the Piedmont produced isolated storms in the late evening hours, which produced some large hail and wind damage in Stanly County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was observed. Additionally, a tree was blown down in a yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward moving cold front out of Virginia stalled out near the North Carolina border. Storms from the late evening on the 15th persisted into the early morning hours of the 16th along and south of the front, producing isolated flash flooding. The stalled boundary then fueled additional numerous showers and storms in the afternoon and evening hours of the 16th along small scale boundaries. Several downed trees and flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old Smithfield Road at Friday Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure across Missouri brought deep and copious moisture across the southeastern United States and the Carolinas. Scattered to numerous showers and storms developed in the early afternoon and peaked in the early evening hours before waning by the late evening. Storms merged with an inland penetrating sea-breeze, leading to several flash flooding reports in Moore and Stanly Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Indian Mound Road. There were numerous other reports of flooded roads in the area east of US Highway 52 to Lake Tillery, south of Stoney Gap Road, and highway 24/27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atypical early August backdoor cold front settled south through North Carolina, as a large cool area of high pressure centered over the Great Lakes nosed southward into central North Carolina. The passage of multiple upper level disturbances over eastern North America coupled with high moisture content and moderate instability supported numerous showers and storms. Sporadic downed trees and power lines and minor flooding were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down, blocking the roadway near the intersection of Thayer Road and Cashatt Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal zone was oriented southwest to northeast across central North Carolina. Scattered showers and storms developed during the late afternoon and evening within a highly anomalous moisture environment. Some training storms led to several instances of flash flooding over the eastern and northeastern Piedmont, especially across Wake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pigeon House Creek at Fenton Street exceeded minor flood stage, with impacts of inundated buildings adjacent to Capital Boulevard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported across Coley Store Road. The road was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough approaching the Mid South and a slow moving surface cold front across western and northern North Carolina kept central North Carolina in a deep and moist southwest flow. Afternoon heating in the east contributed to a few fast-moving bowing segments that produced severe straight-line winds and led to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven large trees and a few powerlines were reported down, blocking roads in the Laurel Hill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the southern Appalachian region on April 6th, exiting the area to the east during the morning hours on April 7th. This front produced several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall in the mountains of east Tennessee and western North Carolina. This rainfall caused flooding along Tusquittee Creek near Hayesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stamey Cove Road was washed out earlier this morning by the flooded Tusquittee Creek. Water approached at least one house, flooding the front yard. Tusquittee Creek Road is closed along with a reported 7 other roads in Clay County. Creek gauge near Hayesville crested at 19.8 feet at 8:50 AM. Pictures shared via Facebook show Pine Log Creek has also escaped its banks and flooded backyards and low-lying lands along the creek. Water appears to be near at least one house in the photo.

An area of heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms developed over the North Carolina Piedmont in association with a surface low and stalled frontal boundary draped over the Coastal Plain. Localized very heavy rainfall resulted in urban and small stream flash flooding in south Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager and stream gauges reported flash flooding developed in south Charlotte, mainly along Little Sugar Creek and Little Hope Creek after 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in just a couple of hours. Little Hope Creek overflowed its banks and inundated part of Mockingbird Lane. Little Sugar Creek flooded a portion of Wakefield Dr, covered a part of Reece Rd with a couple of feet of water, and approached some homes south of Archdale Dr. Emergency manager reported multiple swift water rescues of motorists in south Charlotte. Media reported multiple cars stranded in floodwater in the Pineville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the Appalachian Mountains on March 5. Strong winds aloft were mixed down to the surface from showers and thunderstorms, which caused notable reports of damaging winds that blew down numerous trees and power lines. The most notable damage reported was a roof and a metal carport blown down by the thunderstorm winds in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down one tree on to North Carolina Highway 16 near the Central Wilkes Middle School and one tree on to West Meadows Road about a half of a mile east of North Carolina Highway 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within cool northeasterly surface flow, several upper-level disturbances tracked across central North Carolina from a persistent weak trough of low pressure over the Ohio Valley region. Copious amounts of moisture remained from the 5th of August. A wedge front was also in place along Interstate 95. Several reports of flash flooding were reported over the eastern Piedmont and northern Coastal Plain, including two fatalities in Nash County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people died after their vehicle was swept off North Carolina Highway 581 and into a ravine or ditch that was overflowing with fast moving flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm system that produce a deadly tornado outbreak over the Southeast U.S. and Mid-Mississippi Valley moved across the Carolinas causing some severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed just west of Herrings Chapel Rd about 4 miles east of Currie, NC. As it moved toward the north-northeast the tornado caused tree damage and damage to multiple dog kennels. The tornado then crossed Herrings Chapel Rd causing extensive tree damage in the area. The tornado then weakened as it lifted into wooded areas toward the northeast. Damage was observed again near Fennell Town Rd with houses having some roof damage, along with a few snapped and uprooted trees. The tornado dissipated north of Fennell Town Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep mid-level trough and associated cold front approached from the west on the 16th. Ahead of the trough/front, surface dewpoints in the 60s spread into central North Carolina, with strong low-level and deep-layer shear. Two rounds of severe weather occurred from mid morning through the evening hours. Several reports of damaging winds and large hail were reported, along with isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Hurricane Erin stayed several hundred miles east of the Middle Atlantic coast on August 21, 2025. However, due the extremely large size of the cyclone with an expansive wind field, tropical storm force gusts occurred over the Atlantic, Currituck Sound, and northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although Hurricane Erin passed well offshore on August 21, the wind field was so large that northerly winds gusted to 30 to 40 miles per hour from 3 AM EST through 4 PM EST. The peak gust was 40 mph at the Elizabeth City Regional Airport ASOS around 8 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong mid to upper level low pressure system moved from the southern Plains northeast through the Great Lakes region March 4-5. Ahead of a trailing cold front, a long and nearly continuous line of showers and storms which had held together crossing the Mid South region, pushed into the North Carolina Foothills and western Piedmont in the morning of the 5th, crossing central North Carolina through mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duke energy reported an outage caused by fallen trees or limbs damaging equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong S/SE winds developed across the North Carolina mountains early in the morning in advance of a dynamic frontal system. The strongest winds occurred between 3 AM and 5 AM, possibly assisted by a band of rain with embedded heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms that mixed higher winds from aloft to the valley floors. Some minor structural damage was reported to houses across the region, while numerous trees were blown down, some onto structures, vehicles, and power lines. One tree fell on a trailer in the Conestee community of Transylvania County, killing a 13-year-old male and an 11-year-old male who were sleeping inside. Multiple outbuildings were damaged or destroyed in Haywood County. Power outages numbered in the tens of thousands at the peak of the event. The highest reliable measured gust across the area was at the Asheville Regional Airport, where official NWS obs measured a gust of 59 mph at 422 AM. However, damage reports suggest gusts likely reached as high as 70 mph in some locations. The band of showers and storms strengthened as it moved into the Piedmont later in the morning, with a small area of damaging wind gusts developing in northeast Union County in association with a short line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported (via social media) a couple of trees blown down northeast of Unionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin passed well offshore and combined with high pressure to the north, resulting in a period of strong onshore winds and coastal flooding across portions of the northern North Carolina Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin passed well offshore and combined with high pressure to the north, resulting in a period of strong onshore winds and coastal flooding across portions of the northern North Carolina Outer Banks. The tide gauge at Duck, NC recorded major tidal flooding of 7.04 feet MLLW at 5:42 PM EST on August 21. Moderate flooding of 6.17 feet MLLW was observed at 6:36 PM EST on August 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large trees were blown down by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving thunderstorms developed over western North Carolina during the early morning hours. Multiple slow-moving storms moved over southern Cleveland County, resulting in up to 5 inches of rain falling in a short period of time, followed by localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported flash flooding developed across southern Cleveland County after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell between Earl and Boiling Springs in just a couple of hours. Bowens River reportedyl overflowed its banks near Earl and inundated both lanes of Austell Graham Rd. High flows and stream levels on Shoal Creek also washed out at least one culvert under Shoal Creek Church Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure across Missouri brought deep and copious moisture across the southeastern United States and the Carolinas. Scattered to numerous showers and storms developed in the early afternoon and peaked in the early evening hours before waning by the late evening. Storms merged with an inland penetrating sea-breeze, leading to several flash flooding reports in Moore and Stanly Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Indian Mound Road and Alpine Road. There were numerous other reports of flooded roads in the area east of US Highway 52 to Lake Tillery, south of Stony Gap Road and Highway 24/27.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over the intersection of Lambs Road and North Carolina Highway 343. Water was reported to be ten inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization. A line of storms crossed into northeast North Carolina in the evening, producing scattered instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported multiple trees down across Gaston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northern North Carolina during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of northern North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds near the intersection of Waterford Way and Cody Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward moving cold front out of Virginia stalled out near the North Carolina border. Storms from the late evening on the 15th persisted into the early morning hours of the 16th along and south of the front, producing isolated flash flooding. The stalled boundary then fueled additional numerous showers and storms in the afternoon and evening hours of the 16th along small scale boundaries. Several downed trees and flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down along Fayetteville road west of the town of Falcon.

The largest hail likely fell just to the northeast over the Great Dismal Swamp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across the North Carolina Piedmont during the morning in the vicinity of a weak frontal boundary. An area of flooding developed across southern Rowan County in response to 2.5 to 4 inches of rain falling over several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported stream flooding developed in areas south of Rockwell after 2.5 to 4 inches of rain fell across the area over a period of 4 to 6 hours. Klums Creek overflowed its banks and inundated Old Beatty Ford Rd. Water from a tributary inundated another portion of Old Beatty Ford near Emanual Church Rd. Another tributary overflowed onto Gin Rd near Eller Rd. Water also reportedly covered other roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate rip current activity led to multiple rescues in Oak Island, NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oak Island Water Rescue reported ten rip current rescues made due to moderate rip current activity. Seven rescues were made by emergency personnel while the additional three were made by civilians.

These isolated storms weakened over the eastern sections of central North Carolina. A few storms produced wind damage over Forsyth and Guilford Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and power lines were reported down along Cypress Park Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a warm and humid air mass with weak steering flow but high moisture and instability, a few clusters of storms that started over the northwest Piedmont became more numerous as they tracked east southeast and approached the Triangle, producing areas of strong downdrafts and prompting three severe warnings over and southeast of the Triangle. Flood advisories were also issued, with the main one being over Raleigh. After multiple days of above normal rainfall, including that which produced considerable flooding in Moore county and Chapel Hill, this additional rain led to flash flooding in the usual spots in Raleigh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hundred power outages were reported, due to trees down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Chantal made landfall over northeastern South Carolina during the morning hours of July 6. The remnants of Chantal then tracked west-northwest into portions of central North Carolina, stalling out over the area before tracking east-northeast into Virginia by the 7th of July. Catastrophic flash flooding occurred from record rainfall of up to 11 inches in some spots of the Piedmont, included 6 flash flood fatalities across Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Widespread flooding particularly affected the counties of Orange, Chatman, and Moore. Four EF-1 tornadoes also touched down over portions Lee, Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Preliminary loss estimates across central North Carolina exceeded 40 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aberdeen Creek flooded parts of North Carolina Highway 5 and US Highway 1 with several roads closed in the area. Damage estimates may have exceeded 5 million, including to roads, bridges, infrastructure, and residential areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heatwave impacted northeast North Carolina on July 27. Heat indices reached 110-115 degrees with high temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and dew points in the upper 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heatwave impacted Gates County on July 27. Heat indices reached 110-115 degrees with high temperatures in the low to mid 90s and dew points in the upper 70s to lower 80s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a warm and humid air mass with weak steering flow but high moisture and instability, a few clusters of storms that started over the northwest Piedmont became more numerous as they tracked east southeast and approached the Triangle, producing areas of strong downdrafts and prompting three severe warnings over and southeast of the Triangle. Flood advisories were also issued, with the main one being over Raleigh. After multiple days of above normal rainfall, including that which produced considerable flooding in Moore county and Chapel Hill, this additional rain led to flash flooding in the usual spots in Raleigh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was uprooted and downed partially on a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power outages were reported, due to fallen trees on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Chantal made landfall over northeastern South Carolina during the morning hours of July 6. The remnants of Chantal then tracked west-northwest into portions of central North Carolina, stalling out over the area before tracking east-northeast into Virginia by the 7th of July. Catastrophic flash flooding occurred from record rainfall of up to 11 inches in some spots of the Piedmont, included 6 flash flood fatalities across Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Widespread flooding particularly affected the counties of Orange, Chatman, and Moore. Four EF-1 tornadoes also touched down over portions Lee, Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Preliminary loss estimates across central North Carolina exceeded 40 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down at the Raleigh Executive Jetport 7.5 miles northeast of Sanford in Lee County, North Carolina. Several hangers had damage to wall panels and doors. Several aircraft were damaged. Minor damage to the taxiway occurred. The tornado briefly lifted before touching back down near its end point, and resulted in minor damage to several mobile homes and several trees that were snapped and uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down along Moncure Pittsboro Road and snapped and uprooted several trees, before continuing to the northwest for approximately 1.4 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Bolivia, NC causing it to catch fire with significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Bolivia, NC causing it to catch fire. Damage to the home was significant and all of the homeowner's belongings were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat due to a combination of hot temperatures and high dewpoints led to heat indices peaking above 105 degrees for much of interior Eastern NC the last week of July, centered on July 26th through July 28th, when peak heat indices reached as high as 110 to 115 degrees. Little relief was felt overnight, as lows ranged from the upper 70s to around 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked at 110 to 115 degrees during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong high over the southeastern United States produced very hot and humid conditions during the day with an extreme heat warning over eastern sections of central North Carolina. Heat indices were as high as 115 degrees across the eastern Piedmont, Coastal Plain, and Sandhills of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices ranged from 108 to 112 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Chantal made landfall over northeastern South Carolina during the morning hours of July 6. The remnants of Chantal then tracked west-northwest into portions of central North Carolina, stalling out over the area before tracking east-northeast into Virginia by the 7th of July. Catastrophic flash flooding occurred from record rainfall of up to 11 inches in some spots of the Piedmont, included 6 flash flood fatalities across Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Widespread flooding particularly affected the counties of Orange, Chatman, and Moore. Four EF-1 tornadoes also touched down over portions Lee, Chatham, Alamance, and Orange Counties. Preliminary loss estimates across central North Carolina exceeded 40 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buckhorn Road was closed due to flooding near exit 157 of Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm passed across the Town of West Jefferson, NC during the afternoon of August 20th, and produced rainfall rates briefly of up to 6 inches per hour.  CREST Unit Streamflow during the event maxed out at around 850 cfs per mi^2, while the FLASH 1-hour ARI was observed at 5:20 pm EDT as high as a 25-year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Little Buffalo Creek to overflow its banks several inches deep across South Main  Street in the Town of West Jefferson.  No damage was reported to the road, which was open to traffic after the water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed across the North Carolina foothills and western Piedmont during the afternoon and evening in association with a stationary front. Several storms produced severe weather in the form of locally damaging wind gusts. Additionally, several strong storms trained over the same locations in central McDowell County, resulting in flash flooding in the Marion area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flash flooding developed in Marion and vicinity after 2.5 to 4 inches of rain fell in less than two hours. Flash flooding was first reported in downtown Marion, mainly in the form of  severe urban/drainage flooding. Two buildings were inundated and damaged: one on Henderson St and another on Court St. Youngs Fork overflowed its banks in Marion and overtopped the bridge on Shop Dr. A motorist was rescued on Owl Hollow Rd due to inundation from Jacktown Creek. The most significant impacts occurred at a campground along North Muddy Creek and Bledsoe Branch, where floodwater resulted in three swift water rescues, and evacuation of numerous additional people. Three buildings and multiple recreational and standard vehicles were flooded at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow-moving thunderstorms passed across eastern Surry County and western Stokes County during the evening of August 21st.  Rainfall rates within some of the stronger storms were observed to be as high as 5 inches per hour. The FLASH 3-hour ARI in the Town of King was observed to be as high as a 20-year event at 11 pm EDT, while the 3-hour QPE/FFG ratio was observed reaching 105.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carson Street in Pilot Mountain, NC is was observed flooded around 1 foot deep as runoff from heavy rain overflowed a drainage ditch across the roadway, making the road impassible to traffic.  The road was reopened to traffic after the water receded.
