EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred after the passage of a strong cold front as a pressure gradient increased between a departing low pressure system and a building high pressure system. Gusts of roughly 60 to 70 mph were measured by equipment across the northwest mountains of North Carolina between the late afternoon on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. The strongest measured gust, 71 mph, occurred near Blowing Rock in Watauga County late in the evening on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 59 and 71 mph occurred across parts of Watauga County from the afternoon on the 29th through the early morning hours on the 30th. The strongest gusts were concentrated around the areas of Boone and Blowing Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. Behind this front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds were reported, some of which blew down trees. The strongest reported wind gusts occurred in Watauga County, where a mesonet instrument measured 73 mph near Meat Camp. In Ashe County, a 67 mph gust was recorded by the AWOS at the Ashe County Airport (KGEV), and a 69 mph gust was measured near West Jefferson by a mesonet station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site located one mile east of Piney Creek measured a wind gust of 60 mph at 8:04 AM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 69 mph wind gust was reported by mesonet equipment located one mile north-northeast of West Jefferson at 11:30 AM EST.  However, the wind gusted to 58 mph or greater five additional times that day, the first at 9:00 AM EST, with the last at 7:30 PM EST. The AWOS at the Ashe County Airport at reported a 67 mph wind gusts at 11:35 AM EST, but had been at or above 58 mph for around an 80 minute period starting at 10:35 AM. Later that same day, gusts at the airport again surpassed 58 mph at 4:55 PM EST with periodic gusts around 60 mph until 7:35 PM EST. The high winds also caused a tree to be blown over onto a vehicle along Whenlin Ridge Road, one mile east of Flatwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin made its closest approach to the North Carolina coast during the morning hours of Thursday, August 21st, 2025 as a category 2. While the center of Erin remained around 200 miles east of Cape Hatteras, Erin was so large that damaging ocean overwash and storm surge impacted Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands, and caused additional minor ocean overwash along ocean areas to the north and to the south, and minor soundside flooding on Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands, as well as Downeast Carteret County. According to the National Hurricane Center, Erin was one of the largest hurricanes on record. Additional impacts included dangerous rip currents on Eastern NC beaches, and tropical storm force gusts along the Outer Banks. Erin brought no rainfall or tornado impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major overwash and storm surge significantly impacted Ocracoke Island for several days. A mandatory evacuation was issued for visitors and residents days before the main effects. While some overwash began on August 19th with distant swells, it became substantial on August 20th. Highway 12 on Ocracoke Island experienced severe inundation from ocean overwash and storm surge for several days, leading to beach erosion that exposed the sides of the road, as reported by NCDOT. The section of Highway 12 between the pony pens and the Ocracoke-Hatteras ferry terminal remained closed until 5:00 PM on Monday, August 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin made its closest approach to the North Carolina coast during the morning hours of Thursday, August 21st, 2025 as a category 2. While the center of Erin remained around 200 miles east of Cape Hatteras, Erin was so large that damaging ocean overwash and storm surge impacted Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands, and caused additional minor ocean overwash along ocean areas to the north and to the south, and minor soundside flooding on Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands, as well as Downeast Carteret County. According to the National Hurricane Center, Erin was one of the largest hurricanes on record. Additional impacts included dangerous rip currents on Eastern NC beaches, and tropical storm force gusts along the Outer Banks. Erin brought no rainfall or tornado impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major ocean overwash and surge occurred for several days on Hatteras Island. Hatteras Island was under a mandatory evacuation for visitors and residents several days before significant impacts were noted. Some overwash occurred during the high tide cycle on the afternoon of August 19th as distant swell began impacting the Outer Banks. As the storm moved closer, overwash became significant on August 20th, and completely inundated Highway 12 in several sections on Hatteras Island for several days. Areas that became impassable due to severe ocean overwash include near Hatteras Village, Buxton, and the Pea Island Visitor Center area. Highway 12 on Hatteras Island did not fully reopen until noon on Saturday, August 23rd. The surge and overwash peaked on August 21st, as Erin made her closest approach to Cape Hatteras. Damages sustained to properties during the storm were primarily due to storm surge and ocean overwash. The damage was localized to the districts of Rodanthe and Buxton primarily along the oceanfront, with Buxton sustaining the more substantial damage. In Buxton, preliminary damage assessments from the county found damage to 56 residential structures and 4 commercial structures including driveways, parking lots, as well as septic systems and pipes. It should be noted that some minor sound side flooding also Occurred on Hatteras Island due to the northerly tropical storm force winds on the morning of August 21st, but no significant impacts occurred on the sound side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast winds across northeast North Carolina on October 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed due to a coastal low lingering offshore on October 12. Winds frequently gusted to 40-50 mph between 3 AM and 4 PM EST on October 12. There was a peak wind gust of 51 mph at the Carova Beach Fire Department mesonet and 40 mph at the Currituck County Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Distant Hurricanes Humberto Imelda moved well off the East Coast at the end of the month, but produced major ocean overwash and wave runup affecting Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands for several days at the end of the month and into the beginning of October due to large long period swells. Highway 12 became impassable around high tide on September 30th, and remained closed until the early evening of October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Distant Hurricanes Imelda and Humberto combined to produce large long period swells that inundated parts of Ocracoke Island. Highway 12 was closed for several days between the Pony Pens and the northern ferry terminal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed offshore of the Southeast coast in the early morning of October 28. This system lifted northeastward along a frontal boundary later in the day, while strengthening some. High pressure north of the region led to a compressed pressure gradient with gusty winds across coastal portions of northeast North Carolina for most of the day. Peak wind gusts were in the 45-50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An offshore low pressure system brought gusty northeast winds to coastal areas October 28. A peak observed wind gust of 50 mph (43 knots) was recorded at the Carova Beach Fire Department station. Winds decreased in the evening hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An offshore low pressure system brought gusty northeast winds to coastal areas October 28. A peak observed wind gust of 50 mph (43 knots) was recorded at the Carova Beach Fire Department mesonet station. Peak wind gusts were lower, around 40 mph, in inland portions of Currituck County. However, estimated wind gusts of 45 mph were observed in eastern portions of the county. Winds decreased in the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong coastal storm and king tides produced moderate to major coastal flooding across parts of coastal Eastern NC. Sound side areas of the southern Pamlico Sound and Neuse River experienced coastal flooding due to a prolonged duration of strong northeast to east winds. Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands experienced major coastal flooding due to large long periods swells and king tides. Some flooding, albeit not as severe, was observed on the northern Outer Banks as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm produced high winds and large waves on the Outer Banks through the weekend and into Monday, October 13th. The compromised dune structures from previous storms allowed for ocean overwash to easily overtake Highway 12 in multiple areas on Highway 12, prompting the closure of the roadway during the hours surrounding the high tide cycles. The storm coincided with King Tides, exacerbating the coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong 1035mb area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast to north winds across the North Carolina coastal waters, which allowed for moderate to major coastal flooding across northeast North Carolina October 12 into October 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast from October 11 through October 13 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across portions of northeast North Carolina. This led to several rounds of moderate to major coastal flooding across northeast North Carolina from October 12 into October 14. Duck reached 6.93 ft MLLW at 1130 AM EST on October 12, 6.34 ft MLLW at 1236 PM EST on October 13, and 6.21 ft MLLW at 136 PM EST on October 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nor'easter developed off the South Carolina coast. The low was able to tap into tropical moisture leading to very heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Railroad St was completely flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm produced high winds and large waves on the Outer Banks through the weekend and into Monday, October 13th. The compromised dune structures from previous storms allowed for ocean overwash to easily overtake Highway 12 between the pony pens and northern Ocracoke Island ferry terminal, prompting the closure of the roadway for several days. The storm coincided with King Tides, exacerbating the coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions were in place across Martin, Pitt, and Greene Counties from early October through the end of the month according to the US Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were noted across over half of Pitt County from October seventh through the end of the month according to the US drought monitor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought Conditions were noted across all of Greene County from October seventh through the end of the month according to the US Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system moved offshore and combined with high pressure to the north to produce strong northeasterly winds, which in turn brought large waves and ocean overwash to Ocracoke and Hatteras Islands from October 27-29. The resultant impacts caused five oceanfront homes to collapse, and closed sections of N.C. 12 on Hatteras and Ocracoke Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ocean overwash closed sections of N.C. 12 near between the pony pens and the north end of Ocracoke Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-pressure system moved offshore and combined with high pressure to the north to produce strong northeasterly winds, which in turn brought large waves and ocean overwash to Ocracoke and Hatteras Islands from October 27-29. The resultant impacts caused five oceanfront homes to collapse, and closed sections of N.C. 12 on Hatteras and Ocracoke Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five oceanfront homes collapsed in Buxton and closed sections of N.C. 12 near the Pea Island Visitor Center near the high tide cycles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast winds across northeast North Carolina on October 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed due to a coastal low lingering offshore on October 12. Winds frequently gusted to 40-50 mph between 12 AM and 8 PM EST on October 12. Eastern Currituck had a peak wind gust of 51 mph at the Carova Beach Fire Department mesonet. A peak gust to 55 mph occurred just to the south at the Duck NOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front advanced through the region during the early morning hours of the 19 December. A large area of light to moderate rain moved into central North Carolina during the evening of the 18 December. This rain moved out during the overnight hours. As the cold front advanced eastward, a narrow line of storms tracked east from the Piedmont and Triad into the Triangle and northern Coastal Plain, before clearing out during the mid-morning hours. Several trees and powerlines were reported down across Randolph, Stanly, Chatham, Moore, Alamance, Orange, and Wake Counties. One tree fell through a roof of a home, but no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down in the town of Gibsonville, near the Guilford and Alamance County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an upper level disturbance and plenty of cold air at and near the surface, allowed for the development of a widespread area of snow. Within this broader region of snow, heavier snow bands developed allowing for locally higher amounts. Across the mountains of northwest North Carolina, snowfall amounts ranged from three to seven inches. The high end of this range occurred in and around Boone in Watauga County. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall occurred across the county during the pre-dawn hours through the early afternoon of the 8th. An average of five inches was reported across the county, specifically at both Barrett and a location one mile south-southwest of Glade Valley. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred after the passage of a strong cold front as a pressure gradient increased between a departing low pressure system and a building high pressure system. Gusts of roughly 60 to 70 mph were measured by equipment across the northwest mountains of North Carolina between the late afternoon on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. The strongest measured gust, 71 mph, occurred near Blowing Rock in Watauga County late in the evening on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 65 mph were reported at a location one mile east of Piney Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season arctic front swept through the southern Appalachians late on the 9th, with snow levels falling quickly in the front's wake throughout the early morning of the 10th. The cold air interacted with moisture banked against the western slopes in northwest upslope flow to produce numerous snow showers that moved across mainly the Tennessee border counties throughout the 10th into the early morning of the 11th, resulting in the first notable snowfall of the season. Accumulations below about 3500 feet were generally in the 1-3 range, with some higher amounts along the Tennessee border. The higher elevations along the state line saw 5-10 with locally higher amounts of a foot or more on the high peaks.

Light rain developed across the southern Appalachians during the early morning hours in association with a frontal system over the Tennessee Valley. Pockets of cold air trapped along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge resulted in some of this precip falling as freezing rain across the northern mountains. Total ice accretion of up to one tenth of inch is reported in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong post-frontal cold air advection and a record cold and strong upper level trough resulted in widespread low temperatures in the middle 20s to lower 30s on the morning of November 11 across all of central North Carolina. This hard freeze ended the growing season for the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures were below freezing across the county, including a reported low of 29 degrees at the Burlington-Alamance Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across the Mid-Atlantic early on July 2. In the afternoon, the front slowly advanced southward, serving as a focus for showers and storms across northeast North Carolina through the day. Storms were slow-moving, had high rain rates, and trained over locations for an extended duration. This led to scattered instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported standing water on Route 12 south of Corolla. Water was two to three inches deep on a few sections of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was anchored north of the area in Pennsylvania. Deep moisture was in place to the south of the front with precipitable water values near two inches. Strong surface heating resulted in robust instability with wind shear around 25 knots, allowing for deep convection with strong downdraft winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell along NC 137.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season arctic front swept through the southern Appalachians late on the 9th, with snow levels falling quickly in the front's wake throughout the early morning of the 10th. The cold air interacted with moisture banked against the western slopes in northwest upslope flow to produce numerous snow showers that moved across mainly the Tennessee border counties throughout the 10th into the early morning of the 11th, resulting in the first notable snowfall of the season. Accumulations below about 3500 feet were generally in the 1-3 range, with some higher amounts along the Tennessee border. The higher elevations along the state line saw 5-10 with locally higher amounts of a foot or more on the high peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Distant Hurricanes Humberto Imelda moved well off the East Coast at the end of the month, but produced major ocean overwash and wave runup affecting Hatteras and Ocracoke Islands for several days at the end of the month and into the beginning of October due to large long period swells. Highway 12 became impassable around high tide on September 30th, and remained closed until the early evening of October 2nd. A total of 5 homes collapsed in one day in Buxton on September 30th. Damages sustained to properties during the storm were primarily due to ocean overwash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Distant Hurricanes Imelda and Humberto combined to produce large long period swells that inundated parts of Ocracoke Island. Highway 12 was closed for several days between the Pony Pens and the northern ferry terminal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple clusters of thunderstorms developed and moved southeast ahead of a cold front across east Tennessee and western North Carolina throughout the evening. Several storms produced brief severe weather throughout western NC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat due to a combination of hot temperatures and high dewpoints led to heat indices peaking above 105 degrees for much of Eastern NC the last week of June, centered on June 24th through June 25th, when peak heat indices reached as high as 110 to 115 degrees. Little relief was felt overnight, as lows ranged from the upper 70s to around 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked in the 110-115 degree range each afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. This low brought a prolonged period of elevated northerly winds across the Currituck Sound and North Carolina coastal waters, resulting in elevated seas and moderate coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought gusty winds to coastal portions of the northeast North Carolina Outer Banks September 15 into 16. On September 15, the low pressure system was located offshore and southeast of the area, with brief gusty winds to around 45 mph along the immediate coast in the afternoon due to a tightening pressure gradient. Wind gusts dropped below 40 mph from the evening into the early morning hours of September 16. However, winds increased and peaked in the morning and early afternoon September 16 as the low tracked northwest into the lower Chesapeake Bay and eventually moved inland. Peak wind gusts of around 50 mph were observed in coastal Currituck County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increasingly gusty winds developed as a compact, but deepening, low pressure system approached the Virginia coast September 15 into 16. A brief period of wind gusts to around 45 mph occurred along the immediate Currituck County coast in the afternoon of September 15. Occasional wind gusts over 45 mph started in the early morning hours of September 16 and lasted through the early afternoon. A peak wind gust of 48 mph (42 kt) was observed at the Carova Beach Fire Department. Winds quickly decreased later in the later afternoon and evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported flash flooding developed on the north side of Lenoir after 2 to 4 inches of rain fell over the area in less than two hours. The main impacts were to Blair Fork and its tributaries. Blair Fork overflowed its bans and inundated multiple roads, including Main St NW and Valway Rd, where water was up to car doors. Multiple water rescues were reported along Blair Fork. Water from a tributary inundated  portions of Indian Grave Rd and Bill Tuttle Rd, where water entered a mobile home. Zacks Fork Creek also reportedly overflowed its banks onto a section of Zacks Fork Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and small storm clusters developed across the North Carolina Piedmont during the evening. Several of these clusters moved over south Charlotte, where multiple storms moved repeatedly over the same locations. Very heavy rainfall and significant flash flooding resulted. One of the storms also produced hail over Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3/4 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and small storm clusters developed across the North Carolina Piedmont during the evening. Several of these clusters moved over south Charlotte, where multiple storms moved repeatedly over the same locations. Very heavy rainfall and significant flash flooding resulted. One of the storms also produced hail over Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges and the media reported flash flooding developed across south Charlotte after 2.5 to 5 inches of rain fell across the area due to slow-moving, repeating thunderstorms. Most of the major streams in this part of the city overflowed their banks and inundated adjacent roads, greenways, and residential yards, including Little Hope Creek, Edwards Branch, and Little Sugar Creek. The most significant flooding occurred along Steele Creek in the Steele Creek community, which inundated a mobile home park. Multiple manufactured homes were inundated in this area, and multiple vehicles were largely subsumed in water. Around 30 swift water rescues occurred throughout the city during the event, with most of these occurring in the mobile home park. Thirteen people were also rescued from their vehicles near Freedom Park (Little Sugar Creek). McMullen Creek also experienced severe flooding, with water entering the crawl spaces of some homes and cutting off access to others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and small storm clusters developed across the North Carolina Piedmont during the evening. Several of these clusters moved over south Charlotte, where multiple storms moved repeatedly over the same locations. Very heavy rainfall and significant flash flooding resulted. One of the storms also produced hail over Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain had ended across the Charlotte area by late evening, flooding persisted along multiple streams in the south Charlotte area as flows remained elevated due to the torrential rainfall that fell earlier in the evening. All streams receded to their banks shortly after midnight.
